title: Treasury Proposes Rule to Implement the GENIUS Act's Requirements to Counter Illicit Finance
summary: FinCEN and OFAC issued a joint proposed rule on April 8, 2026 to implement provisions of the GENIUS Act, establishing anti-money laundering and sanctions compliance requirements for permitted payment stablecoin issuers (PPSIs). The rule would treat PPSIs as financial institutions under the Bank Secrecy Act, requiring them to adopt AML programs and maintain effective sanctions compliance programs.
key_points: FinCEN and OFAC jointly issued the proposed rule to implement GENIUS Act anti-money laundering and sanctions compliance requirements
The rule would designate permitted payment stablecoin issuers (PPSIs) as financial institutions under the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA)
PPSIs would be required to adopt and maintain effective anti-money laundering programs
PPSIs would be required to maintain effective sanctions compliance programs as mandated by the GENIUS Act
The proposed obligations are described as fit-for-purpose, designed to assist law enforcement while minimizing burden
Public comments are welcome; the proposal will be published in the Federal Register in the coming days
Secretary Bessent stated the proposal aims to protect the U.S. financial system from national security threats without hindering payment stablecoin innovation
why_it_matters: This proposed rule establishes the regulatory framework for payment stablecoin oversight in the U.S., creating compliance obligations that will shape how stablecoin issuers operate. The requirements could affect the cost and feasibility of launching or maintaining payment stablecoins, with potential implications for adoption and market structure in the digital asset space. This represents a significant step in federal regulation of the stablecoin ecosystem.
macro_context: The GENIUS Act provides the framework for federal regulation of payment stablecoins. This proposed rule implements the AML/sanctions compliance aspects but does not address other GENIUS Act provisions or broader monetary policy implications.
